data.title: Going Brown: Shorting DSI To Bet Against ESG
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Phaseshift Research as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction

The new U.S. Administration has assumed its functions only a few weeks ago, notwithstanding this, some important, new policy trends are apparent in budgetary matters and in the real economy. One segment of the economy affected by these policy shifts is Environment, Social, Governance [ESG]. In this context, we may refer to the already-taken decisions of pulling out of the Paris Climate Agreement, slashing USAID spending and further deregulation and defunding in line with the above. These measures, along with others that are already in the pipeline, will inevitably impact ESG-based equity valuations. While having no judgment whatsoever regarding the policy shifts in Washington, it is clear that a possibility is opening to capitalize on the idea of shorting the iShares ESG MSCI KLD 400 ETF (NYSEARCA:DSI) and longing the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) to isolate and bet against the ESG factor.

Thesis

There is a clear and escalating pattern of ESG-abandonment, and there are serious reasons to believe that this trend will at least persist, if not strengthen, over the next four years.

It appears that there is broad GOP support for the decisions of the new Administration to roll-back environmental regulation as well as Diversity, Equality and Inclusion [DEI] programs. On 28 January 2025 Republican-Party leaning officials (auditors, comptrollers and treasurers) from 18 states co-signed a letter to the Securities and Exchange Commission [SEC] urging for the prohibition of ESG asset management mandates. The new Department of Government Efficiency [DOGE], and its first steps in the direction of institutional ESG defunding and regulation, is a sign that points towards the long-term nature of the shift, rather than a short-term campaign. Symptomatically, major private-sector players have been following down a similar path. Since 2023, BlackRock CEO, Larry Fink, once a vehement ESG proponent, has conspicuously dropped the term entirely. In 2024, J.P. Morgan, along with Goldman Sachs and several other major asset managers, left the Climate Action 100+ alliance. Most recently, in December 2024, the Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals ruled to drop Nasdaq’s board diversity rules.

Strategy

To capitalize on the above-described policy shift, it is possible to isolate and bet against the ESG factor. To do this, we can short DSI and hedge U.S. market exposure by hedging with a SPY long, re-balancing the hedge on a weekly basis.

DSI is the ticker for the iShares MSCI KLD 400 Social ETF - it seeks to track an index composed of companies that have positive ESG characteristics, drawn from a universe of the 3,000 largest U.S. public equities. SPY is the ticker for the SPDR S&P 500 ETF - and it is a market-cap weighted composition of the 500 largest U.S. public equities.

To attempt to isolate the ESG factor (returns attributable to changes in ESG) we can take a Capital Asset Pricing Model [CAPM]-like approach.

We define SPY as the CAPM market portfolio,

We define DSI as a regular CAPM security with the addition of an idiosyncratic ESG premium,

We define the strategy as an outright short on DSI and a SPY long, levered by DSI’s beta,

Substituting the given values into the equation and simplifying, we obtain:

Which for simplicity can be approximated as:

It should be noted that in the derivation above, we add ESG as an alpha as opposed to a factor; this is because ESG tends to behave as more of a discount/premium than as a true factor. Moreover, in the penultimate step, the first term is discarded, this will result in a very subtle misestimation of the strategy’s returns.

From an implementation standpoint, we start by shorting DSI and longing SPY levered to DSI’s beta, however, as time elapses and the securities’ prices diverge, the hedge will lose its accuracy. For this reason, we must rebalance the strategy on a regular basis (weekly rebalancing represents a reasonable trade-off between the discrete hedging error and transaction fees). Rebalancing simply implies making sure that our SPY size = - DSI size * DSI beta.

Shown above are the cumulative daily returns of DSI, SPY and the aforementioned ESG-short strategy over the last 190 days. The strategy is 3x levered to more closely match the standard deviation of SPY. The historical performance of the strategy is mixed; however, the post-inauguration performance (+7.73%) immediately tells us that we are not the only ones looking to short ESG. Whether 7.73% (2.58% unlevered) is accurately representative of the ongoing structural shifts in ESG investing, is subject to many questions. The impact of longitudinal policy initiatives on ESG are difficult to accurately predict, and in such circumstances allocators frequently act on a more conservative basis. It is reasonable to believe that much of this Administration’s ESG reform is yet to be priced accurately, and is likely being under-priced. Providing price targets is unreasonable as this idea is highly event-driven and presents very high potential magnitudinal variation.

Above, we have the strategy’s daily return distribution along with some distributional characteristics for SPY, DSI and the strategy itself [ESG]. Although, as the age-old adage goes “past returns do not guarantee future results” we can look at the return distribution to try and gauge the long-term behavior of the strategy. The strategy distribution is slightly favorable with a positive skew (large positive returns occur more frequently than large negative returns). Whether a Sharpe ratio of 7.56% can be seen as positive or not is dependent on the investor’s opportunity set and conviction in the trade-idea. A kurtosis (tail thickness) of 2.4 is fine, however, given the positive skew a higher kurtosis would be more favorable. Looking at the post-inauguration metrics, the strategy seems very attractive - although it is unlikely for those statistics to persist for the next four years. All-in-all we have a comfortable distributional setup that allows us to focus on the trade-idea without having to excessively worry about what will happen to the position if the catalysts fall-through.

On the more practical side of the strategy, there are a few considerations, starting with the holding period. Given the fact that this trade-idea leans on the intentions of the current Administration, a four-year holding period is well-grounded. This is not to be confused with the expected holding period, which is highly contingent on the exit-conditions. The key trigger that will result in an exit of the trade is a significant loss of political anti-ESG momentum. Once there are no more new initiatives and less direct pressure, even if the political sentiment around ESG continues to be negative - that sentiment will become the new status quo and this trade idea will be fully priced by the market. The other exit-condition is a change in the course taken by the U.S. Administration regarding ESG, while this is unlikely, it should still be considered.

In order to execute the short leg of the strategy, DSI must be borrowed. Borrowing rates on DSI are typically near-zero for institutional investors and ~0.3% per annum for retail investors (of course, this may differ from broker to broker). SPY’s trailing twelve-month average daily volume is ~53m units and DSI’s is ~110k units. Therefore, liquidity issues are unlikely.

With regard to alternative strategies that create similar exposure we can look to options. One possibility is a relative value spread to isolate the ESG factor (buy DSI puts at the money, buy SPY calls at the money with quantity scaled to match DSI’s beta). If executed correctly this may work even better than the strategy on margin, however, there is one major drawback - time decay. As aforementioned the expected holding period of the strategy is uncertain and there is also doubt as to when the catalysts will materialize. This is a big problem in options, as we have to choose an expiration - too short and the catalysts won’t materialize, too long and our returns will be heavily damped. While there are multi-leg strategies and expiration-selection approaches that may mitigate time risk, they are beyond the scope of this article.

Catalysts & Transmission

There are two key catalysts that will contribute to the withering of ESG:

1) The direct impact of ESG deregulation and de-funding

2) The indirect social impact of an Administration that opts not to pursue ESG-conscious initiatives

Transmission Mechanism:

The direct impact of ESG deregulation and defunding is primarily on the supply side. Companies are currently subject to myriads of ESG regulations and incentive-mechanisms and, if the U.S. Administration follows-through on what it is signaling, there is a long way down.

ESG deregulation and de-funding -> Corporate incentives decrease -> ESG project IRR decreases -> ESG projects provide lower returns -> ESG factor decreases.

Rationale: This mechanism is straight-forward. If corporations are not required or financially remunerated to do something, they have little reason to act. In the same vein, if corporate ESG requirements are laxed and government cheques are thinned, ESG projects will yield lower returns. According to Rob Du Boff at Bloomberg Intelligence: "Deregulation is one lever Republicans can use to kill ESG". Furthermore, allocators and corporations may find aligning themselves with the current administration's sentiment opportunistic; further raising the opportunity cost of pursuing ESG projects.

Transmission Mechanism:

The potential indirect demand-side impact of ESG deregulation and de-funding is distinctive.

ESG deregulation and de-funding -> Social relevance decreases -> Demand decreases -> ESG factor decreases.

Rationale: ESG investments are a highly demand-driven market, and whether ESG integration in financial analysis produces outsized returns is still subject to much speculation. In the words of Dr. John Seo, Co-Founder of Fermat Capital Management: "They're basically taking the worst fears of a hundred years of climate change, upper bound, and then pricing it in as the here and now". Governments' views trickle-down on their nations' constituents. If the U.S. government does not care - Americans will eventually start caring less, and given the demand-driven nature of ESG assets, their prices will naturally decrease.

Risks & Counters

Politically, much uncertainty surrounds the future. There is, however, a geopolitical counterargument to be made against the U.S. veering away from ESG investment - China. China is a behemoth in green technology - from electric vehicles to wind turbines to solar. The U.S. entirely pulling out of ESG essentially defaults their market share of these industries to its biggest competitor. This is a dubious and perhaps a risky move. Therefore, it is unreasonable to expect a full ESG ban or similar.

Strategically, there are several important considerations. We isolate the ESG factor by shorting DSI and longing SPY. This prompts two primary assumptions: SPY represents the true market portfolio; DSI's unique variance accurately and exclusively represents the theoretical ESG factor. Fortunately, it is unlikely that these assumptions are violated too severely, to where the strategy's viability is put into question.

Since we need to hedge-out U.S. market equity exposure and constant re-hedging is impossible, we risk incorrectly tracking the ESG factor as the ETFs' prices change. Furthermore, the ETFs themselves may not accurately weight the constituent securities. The use of leverage to match SPY's return volatility is not necessary, however, in the delineated example it poses various risks, such as margin risk and amplified losses. The short position on DSI also poses the risk of an unbounded downside.

Transmission mechanism-wise, it is important to consider that U.S. corporate equity is not solely held by U.S. based investors and approximately 17% is held internationally. While the rest of the world is also impacted by U.S. policy, allocators are ultimately still driven by their own nations' governments, which may still provide some incentives to remain ESG-aligned. Moreover, the link between deregulation/de-funding and social relevance is extremely complex, and the strength and lags of that transmission are certainly up for debate. Ultimately, everything will depend on how aggressively the current Administration will choose to act.

Notwithstanding some uncertainties and marginal risks, the radical changes in policy being instituted by the new U.S. Administration clearly represent opportunities for alpha generation.

data.title: Exact Sciences: A Strong Q4 Reveals Signs Of A Potential Inflection Point
data.description:
It has been over a year since my last Exact Sciences (NASDAQ:EXAS) article from August 2023, when I highlighted the company's encouraging Q2 results, including a 24% year-over-year growth in core revenue and a 31% bump in screening revenue. In addition, Exact upgraded their full-year guidance, however, the market responded negatively, dropping the share price from around $96 to about $82 per share. Although I was a bit apprehensive about their ability to sustain that growth trajectory, I still had a bullish outlook for the company due to the fundamentals and a strong performance from their flagship product, Cologuard. Since then, I have watched EXAS continue to struggle to arrange a strong reversal setup below my Buy Threshold of $62 per share. As a result, the ticker has been gathering dust in my Compounding Healthcare “Bio Boom” speculative portfolio. Admittedly, I have been content with leaving my EXAS in a “House Money” condition as the new administration begins to implement their own policies that could have a dramatic impact on the healthcare sector’s near-term and long-term outlook.

However, I had been looking forward to the company’s Q4 earnings report to see if they were able to meet the Street’s projections and any updates on their 2025 guidance. Well, Exact posted mixed earnings with a solid beat on revenue, but a big miss on EPS. The market did not react to the headline numbers positively, with the share price going from roughly $50 per share down to $46 per share in the subsequent trading session. Indeed, the big miss on EPS did cause some whiplash, a deeper dive revealed that the company has continued to make progress in their core business, and they are expecting strong growth in 2025. Therefore, I am going to keep an eye on EXAS in the coming days and weeks to see if the ticker can finally provide a quality reversal setup that will entice me to take the position out of mothballs.

I intend to review the company’s Q4 earnings and will provide my views on the quarter. In addition, I will take a look at Exact’s 2025 guidance and see how that stacks up to the Street’s expectations. In addition, I point out a few notable downside risks that investors should consider when managing their EXAS position. Finally, I will attempt to figure out if EXAS is worthy of a buy at these valuations and will try to identify any opportunities for me to add to my dormant EXAS position.

Q4 Review

Taking a look at Exact’s Q4 earnings report, the company’s total revenue $713M, which was up 10% year-over-year and up 11% year-over-year on a core basis. The company screening revenue was up 14% to $553M and their Precision Oncology revenue was up slightly to $161M. The company’s adjusted EBITDA of $75M, up $26M over Q4 of 2023. The company also reported their gross margin was 69% with adjusted coming in at 72%. Looking at the cash flow, the company reported that their operating cash flow $47M, with their free cash flow hitting $11M.

Turning to the sticker shock item… the company reported a net loss of $865M, which led to -$4.67 per share, whereas they reported a $49.8M net loss (-$0.27 per share) in Q4 of last year. This was due to $838M impairment charges related to the Thrive acquisition.

In terms of their cash position, the company left 2024 with $1.04B cash, equivalents, and marketable securities.

In addition to the financials, Exact also discussed some important updates, including that they have secured Medicare pricing for Cologuard Plus, which is set to launch with coverage and guideline inclusion next quarter. Exact is also planning on launching Oncodetect in Q2 after a reimbursement submission, along with Cancerguard EX in the second half of this year.

My Take On The Quarter

I did not need to dig very deep to realize that the market's negative reaction to Exact’s Q4 earnings was overblown. Admittedly, the headline EPS figure was shocking, but this was largely due to an $838M impairment charge from the Thrive acquisition. I have to imagine this was a pending issue and might have been factored into the share price at one point in time. In addition, this impairment charge appears to be a one-time event tied to specific circumstances rather than an outcome of the company’s performance.

The company's 10.2% year-over-year revenue growth did exceed expectations, and strong demand for the Cologuard brand suggests their core operations are gaining momentum. Furthermore, the adjusted EBITDA of $75M, along with an improved margin of 11%, suggests the company is growing efficiency and making progress towards moving to profitability.

In addition, the company’s strong cash position of $1.04B and positive cash flow figures should provide some assurance the company's financials are strong enough to allow them to maintain their growth initiatives and keep their pipeline moving. The company expects to launch a few new products this year, so having the finances and established commercial infrastructure should allow those programs to launch smoothly and help accelerate growth in the coming years.

Again, I understand why the market is going to fixate on the negative headline numbers, but the remaining components of the report suggest that Exact is ready for growth and will be able to defend their position as a leading brand in the cancer diagnostics market.

Reviewing the 2025 Guidance

Although 2024 wasn’t a strong year for Exact Sciences, the company is confident about this year and has set ambitious revenue guidance for 2025, with total revenues between $3.025B and $3.085B, which would be up 11% year-over-year. The company anticipates the upcoming launch of Cologuard Plus in Q2 to fuel growth in screening revenue, which is estimated to fall between $2.35B and $2.39B.

In addition, Exact has set their adjusted EBITDA guidance to $410M-$440M, up from $323M in 2024.

Can They Do It?

Well, in addition to some of the pending growth drivers, I would also point to the company’s track record for growth. Exact has reported year-over-year revenue growth for each quarter since Q1 of 2020 often exceeding the Street’s expectations.

Although the earnings per share frequently misses expectations, the general earnings trend suggests the company is heading towards sustained profitability once they get their efficiency and costs under control.

The Street does expect Exact to continue to report strong revenue growth into the next decade, nearly doubling their revenue by 2034.

More importantly, the Street expects the company to report a positive EPS for 2025 and maintain a positive EPS through 2034.

So, one could argue that the company’s optimistic 2025 guidance is a signal that we are in the midst of a significant inflection point in the Exact’s history.

Risks to Consider

Despite my bullish views, I must point out some risks that investors should consider when managing their positions. First, Exact has struggled to make a strong and durable push toward profitability over the past few years, despite nearly doubling their revenue since 2020. Certainly, I understand that the company needs to spend to grow, and the general trend has been up. Plus, we need to consider the impact of COVID on their base business. However, one must concede the erratic earnings do not translate well to the share price. Despite being the name brand in colon cancer screening, Exact appears to have a setback right as they seem ready to cross the threshold of profitability. The company did miss on both revenue and EPS in Q3, which forced the company to amend their 2024 guidance. I believe investors need to accept that EXAS will continue to struggle because the market might not trust the company’s progress until it is almost undeniable.

In addition, investors need to consider the company’s financial health and their ability to get to profitability. The company had roughly $1.04B at the end of 2024, which should be sufficient to get them through 2025. However, the company has a history of erratic cash usage and has an average quarterly cash burn rate of around $86M since Q2 of 2022. The company’s cash runway would be roughly 3 years at that rate. Again, I wouldn’t say their cash position is an immediate threat. However, the pending launches could change that outlook. An underwhelming launch or lackluster growth could have the Street turning on Exact in the coming quarters as the likelihood the company could require a secondary offering to bridge them to profitability.

Considering these risks, I am maintaining my EXAS conviction level at 3 out of 5 and the ticker will remain in the Compounding Healthcare “Bio Boom” speculative portfolio for the foreseeable future.

My Plan

I am looking to turn the market's initial reaction into an opportunity to grab some shares of EXAS in anticipation the company will begin to make a strong push toward profitability and deliver some long-term value. The company has pending launches in 2025 that could be the fuel to sustain long-term growth into the next decade.

Finding a fair price for EXAS can be challenging, but using the price-to-sales model with Exact’s 2034 projected revenue of $5.94B and the industry’s average price-to-sales ratio of 4x to 5x, we can say that the company could be valued between $23.76B and $29.7B, which be about $128-$160 per share. Indeed, that is what the company could be valued at nearly a decade from now, but a fair value in 2025 can be estimated if we discount that valuation for nine years at a 10%-12% rate, giving us $45 to $68 per share. The ticker is trading towards the lower end of that range, around $46-$50 per share. So, I am eyeing for an opportunity to click the buy button.

Looking at the Daily Chart, we can see that EXAS has been under strong selling pressure after their Q3 earnings report in November, and is now trading below my Buy Level 1 of $52 per share.

The share price bounced off $46 per share, forming a nice hammer candle under elevated trading volume. In addition, we can see a bullish divergence in the RSI. So, I believe there are some ingredients for a potential reversal here. However, the ticker is very bearish on the go-no-go indicator and is trading below two anchored-VWAPs. Plus, the downtrend ray from the November high remains overhead.

So, I am going to wait to see what the market is going to do with EXAS in the coming days and weeks. If the ticker remains at these levels for another week, I will consider making a small addition based on the valuation. Then, I will wait for the ticker to break the downtrend ray and climb above the anchored VWAPs before committing to another buy below my “Buy Threshold” of $62 per share.

On the other hand, if the ticker continues to sell off, I will wait for a retest of the June 2024 lows of around $41 per share to formulate a new strategy.

Once I have executed my buy orders, I will set equivalent sell orders at my Sell Targets in order to book some profits and move my position back to a “House Money” status to hold for a long-term investment.

data.title: EURJPY Market Analysis: ECB, BOJ, And Technical Outlook
data.description:
Originally published on February 22, 2025

By Moheb Hanna

ECB Rate Decision: Inflation, Tariffs, and Market Sentiment Impact on EURJPY

The European Central Bank (ECB) is scheduled to announce its interest rates and monetary policy statement on March 6th, 2025, followed by its standard press conference. The meeting occurs during a period of heightened market uncertainty due to trade wars and tariff implications. The Eurozone inflation indicator, the Harmonized Index of Consumer Prices (HICP), rose from its low of 1.7% in September 2024 (a 5-year low) to 2.5% in December 2024. This increase in HICP was mainly due to rising costs in housing, energy, and transportation. The Core HICP remained steady at 2.7% during the same period.

(EZ HICP - Source: Eurostat)

The increase in inflation, along with concerns about the impact of US tariffs on Eurozone inflation, may affect market sentiment. According to Bloomberg analyst surveys, 97.5% of participants expect the ECB to cut rates by 25 basis points at its March 6th, 2025, meeting. This is down from 135.0% on January 30th, 2025, when markets anticipated a possible 50 basis point cut.

Japanese Yen (JPY) Strength: Inflation, Interest Rates, and BOJ Policy Impact on USDJPY

The Japanese yen has gained some strength against the US dollar in early 2025, rising approximately 6% as of early January 2025. While 6% is a significant move in currency trading, it is small compared to the yen’s 35% weakness against the US dollar, which began in early 2021 and ended in July 2024.

(Source: TradingView.com)

The recent upward move was supported by a slower-than-anticipated interest rate cut path by the Federal Reserve and an increase in Japan’s inflation to a 2-year high of 4%, suggesting that BOJ’s rate hikes could be on the horizon. The Bank of Japan (BOJ) policy rate decision is scheduled for March 18th, 2025, a day before the FOMC statement and the federal funds rate announcement. Last week, Bank of Japan Governor Kazuo Ueda stated in parliament that the “BOJ would buy bonds nimbly if yields rise sharply.” According to Bloomberg analyst surveys, expectations for a 25-basis point interest rate hike at the BOJ’s March 18th, 2025 meeting stand at only 1.8%, down from 38.7% in December 2024.

EURJPY Technical Analysis: Ascending Channel Breakout, Head and Shoulders Pattern, and Flag Formation

(Source: TradingView.com)

EURJPY Weekly Japanese Candlestick Chart

  • EURJPY has been trading within an ascending channel since mid-2020. Price action broke and closed below the channel, followed by two pullbacks (PB1 and PB2). Both attempts met resistance along the channel border extension. Two more pullbacks followed (PB3 and PB4), but a shortfall occurred as the price was unable to reach the channel border. The four pullbacks together completed a complex head and shoulders pattern, which had already broken below its neckline and reached its technical target.
  • The overall price action following the breakout below the ascending channel completed a flag formation, a continuation chart pattern. (The flag chart pattern is highlighted in yellow.)
  • A potential exhaustion downward gap formation took the price down to the head and shoulders technical target at the market open on February 3rd, 2025, following President Trump’s tariff announcement regarding Canada and Mexico.
  • Currently, price action is at a confluence of support represented by the lower side of the flag formation and support level S1 of 157.07, where it previously found support in September 2024. (Purple arrows)
  • Price is trading below its moving averages (EMA9, SMA9, and SMA20). The three moving averages intersect with the gap formation near the monthly pivot point of 161.88, forming a confluence of resistance above the price action.
  • The default RSI 14 is at level 40 and approaching oversold territory, and the MACD line remains below its signal line.

Considering the confluence of technical factors, including the ascending channel breakout, head and shoulders pattern, and flag formation, along with the fundamental factors of ECB and BOJ policy decisions and inflation rates, EURJPY presents a complex trading landscape. Traders should closely monitor key support and resistance levels, moving averages, and momentum indicators like RSI and MACD. Robust risk management strategies are essential to navigate the potential volatility and capitalize on opportunities while mitigating downside risk.

Original Post

data.title: Inditex: Zara's Moat, How Taking Design Risks Leads To Superior Margins
data.description:
Two years after my initial analysis of Industria de Diseño Textil, S.A. (OTCPK:IDEXY, OTCPK:IDEXF), the company has returned a total of 173%. Given that the company’s business model hasn’t changed much, you can refer to my initial article above for a deeper breakdown of the business.

The conclusion of my new analysis is that while Inditex is trading at a premium, the stock is still investable for long-term investors due to its growing e-comm business, and relative business model superiority over peers which is allowing it to derive higher margins.

Taking Risks In Design: How Inditex Became A Trend-Setter In The Apparel Industry

Inditex is one of the largest apparel retailer, with 65% of revenues coming from Europe. This is important for investors seeking to diversify revenue sources in their portfolio.

While Inditex is not a high-growth company, it has consistently delivered results above the inflation rate, and is arguably producing a consumer staple which gives the company a cash flow moat.

The company has a high-turnover unique design approach, where inventory rotates every two weeks across stores, giving customers increased options and maintaining a novelty effect. This creates buying pressure by maintaining a sense of urgency, as customers know that a piece they are considering will shortly leave the shelves.

While the high-rotation strategy seems to imply higher manufacturing costs - due to not having a seasonally optimized manufacturing process; it allows the company to view its designs as a part of a diversified portfolio and benefit from quick A/B testing. The result is a high design turnover, leading to a quicker identification of best-selling items. In other words, sticking with a single spring collection would be cheaper because of better allocation of resources, but constantly changing designs allows the company to discover what customers find more appealing and push up the successful designs after a few rotations.

Because of Inditex’s design approach, I view the company as a trend setter, which further increases their pricing power. An interesting remark I’ve heard about Inditex, specifically the Zara brand, is that “you can find seasonal designs in Zara, but you can’t find Zara designs at other retailers.” By the time other retailers bring-in collections that are in sync with new trends, Inditex is already rolling out new lines. This has nothing to do with speed, but a level of risk-taking in the design process that puts Inditex in front of the competition, and gives them the resulting brand power. As a retailer, you could optimize for sales and lower your risk, but then you also have to be content with lower margins, as the best-selling seasonal designs are widely available in the market. In my opinion, the increased risk-taking in design is part of the reason why the company has been a performer in the market.

Growth Avenues & Financial Performance

Inditex is pushing for growth via 2 avenues, increases in store space (more stores or more space utilized per store) and e-commerce.

Balancing Physical Stores

By providing access to nice, and sometimes outright grand stores, Inditex is increasing the social status of their customers. The decently priced items also give them a positive shopping experience with the therapeutic assurance that customers have "made it" – they can walk into a nice store and afford to buy quality items. In my view, stores are a part of the value signaling, as they are designed at a grand-enough level, that makes the items a customer purchases look cheap in comparison.

On the store count side, it seems that the company is balancing its current portfolio and cutting out low-performers. This will impact growth, but also lead to a more stable business, which is a net positive.

By Q3 2024, Inditex cut the store count by 1% to 5659 stores, with the largest closures happening at their Oysho (41) and Zara (36) brands.

E-commerce Development Is Lacking

In 2023, 25% of the sales were made online. Their channels are typically divided into their own brand e-comm sites, and online distributors such as Zalando.

Unfortunately, at least in my view, the online shopping experience is lacking. For example, Zara’s site (tested both the GB and DE version), seems to work better on mobile devices, and tends to randomly revert back to the top of the page. On a wide screen, the design looks unfinished, and the font size seems disproportionate to the images. Also, the site brought me back to the home page after I clicked accept on a cookie banner while I was browsing the store – This is clearly not the intended behavior.

In my view, I don’t see how a channel that is part of the digital basket that made €9.1B in 2023 can be below par relative to their other brands. While some aspects of the digital store are clearly Zara’s unique digital style, it is also evident that some elements do not function as intended.

Compared to Zara, their other stores: Bershka, Pull&Bear, and to some extent Stradivarius & Oysho, are built at a much better online standard.

Third-party online distributors like Zalando are a great way to increase turnover at initial e-commerce roll-out phases. However, ultimately, the goal of the company would be to drive as much shopping activity on their own e-comm stores and maximize profitability by cutting out any middle-men. Inditex has begun this process, but the company has room for improvement, which also means that there is room to increase the value of the business from developing its e-comm channels.

Financial Performance

Inditex reports financial results in EUR, however in order to build a model that corresponds to the IDEXF & IDEXY tickers, I will use the USD converted value from Seeking Alpha – you can always switch between currencies in their financials section.

In the last 12-months, Inditex reported revenues of $41B, growing 10.5% from the previous 12-month period ending in October 2023. The company had an operating profit of $8B, representing a 19.5% margin. While the company didn't give specific growth guidance, analysts expect revenue around $40.6B in 2025 and $44.8B in 2026, equivalent to a 7.7% CAGR.

By the end of FY 2024, Inditex expects to spend $1.88B in ordinary capex, plus a logistics expansion project worth $942M per annum in 2024 and 2025. The current effective tax rate is around 22.5%.

Regarding the bottom line, Inditex posted a net income of $6.2B and unlevered free cash flows of $6.45B. It is a good sign of efficiency to see both the free cash flows and earnings come up close to each other.

Valuation Update

The current PE for Inditex is 28.4x, and its EV to unlevered FCF is 26.4x. These correspond to a 3.5% and 3.8% yield respectively, while the dividend yield was 2.9%.

Starting from the latest $41B of TTM revenues, I expect Inditex to grow revenues to $44B by 2026. I project a decelerating growth trajectory that converges on $72B in revenues in year 10. My rationale for this is that I expect the company to keep growing above inflation in its corresponding markets, and that its brand name and trend setting approach will maintain their appeal.

On the profitability side, I view the company as being on the high side for its industry, but still having some room for margin increases as it works on e-commerce which can enable better inventory management, reduced operating expenses and organic marketing opportunities.

Regarding its capital expenditures, it seems that the company is putting in less than it is depreciating. For FY 2024, the combined capex is expected to total $2.9B, while Inditex is depreciating $3.2B, leading to a negative net capex of $-370M. This approach puts constraints on the company’s long-term growth potential, which is why I am projecting a decelerating growth rate over time.

I estimate a lower, 7.3% cost of capital - primarily due to the 65% of revenues coming from Europe which is pulling the rate down on account of Europe's interest rates which imply a lack of real (above inflation) growth.

Using my estimates, I've constructed a valuation model which leads to a value of the cash flows at $136B. The company has a positive cash position of $9B ($11.9B cash - $3B present value of leases) which I net out and get a final intrinsic value of $145B for Inditex.

The company trades in the U.S. under the tickers IDEXY and IDEXF, for which the intrinsic value per share corresponds to $23 and $47, respectively. In total, my estimated intrinsic value is 17.6% lower than the current market cap is $176B. This means that, purely based on the numbers, the company is trading at a 2.5 year premium, i.e., it will take up to 2.5 years for the fundamentals to catch up to the current price given an estimated return of 7.3% per year.

For investors that have investment horizons that are shorter than this 3-year range, Inditex is not trading at an investable price. For investors that have longer horizons, and diversification goals that include income from Europe as well as increasing the proportion of consumer staples in their portfolio, then Inditex is worth considering. In my view, the added value of Inditex will come from improving profitability from its e-comm channels, implementing higher efficiency stores with self-checkout, and optimizing global logistics, resulting in a decrease in operating costs. I also believe that the company is relatively better than its competitors due to its approach on store design, and the fast-fashion model.

For these reasons, while I don’t expect the same returns I did in my 2022 analysis, I ultimately give Inditex a “buy” rating for investors that are willing to cost average or wait out possible drawdowns.

Risks

  • Moving too aggressively towards recycled input materials can cheapen the item quality and brand. While some demographics may respond positively to wearing recycled materials, this sentiment may not be shared across all target customers within the brand portfolio.

  • Lack of R&D capex may slow down the development of the e-commerce platform and competitors may end up solidifying market share.

  • Despite only a 20% portion of income coming from the U.S., an increased tariff scenario may negatively impact profitability and force management to spend high amounts of capex in order to allocate some production capacity in the U.S. While the tariffs discussion has been naggingly present in my latest articles. My view is that the market is underestimating its impact, as companies are used to having years to adapt to changes – but that is not the world we live in anymore. It is becoming apparent that tariffs will be immediate, enforced, and impactful to the P/L of many companies.

  • The market may change its sentiment on Inditex, and the stock may converge down to intrinsic value.

data.title: Block: I Have Been Waiting For This Drop
data.description:
Cash App-related momentum once again carried Block's (NYSE:XYZ) financial results in the fourth fiscal quarter, yet the Fintech's results missed consensus bottom and top line estimates. However, Block saw double-digit top line growth in both of its core segments, Cash App and Square, and the Fintech benefited from a continual upsurge in gross profits. I believe shares of the Fintech remain an attractive deal for growth investors, especially as more money is flowing into Block’s Cash App ecosystem. Shares of Block crashed 18% after the Fintech submitted its earnings scorecard, which now allows investors to buy Block at about half the historical P/E ratio.

Previous rating

I rated shares of Block a strong buy in November 2024 -- A Cheap And Solid Fintech Play -- due to what I considered was an attractive investment setup: the Fintech was growing rapidly and Cash App Card, a key feature of Block’s Cash App ecosystem, benefited from strong customer acquisition and adoption trends. Block continued to see double-digit growth momentum in both the Block and Square segments in the fourth fiscal quarter. I believe investors overreacted to the bottom and top line miss, and I continue to see a ton of revaluation potential for the Fintech's shares in FY 2025 and beyond.

Block beats estimates

Block missed top and bottom line expectations for the fourth fiscal quarter on Thursday: the Fintech reported non-GAAP earnings of $0.71 per-share, which missed the consensus estimate by $0.16 per-share. Block’s top line was reported at $6.03B, falling short of the consensus estimate by $228M. As a result, shares of Block skidded 18% on Friday as investors worried about the Fintech's growth.

Surging gross profits, led by Cash App, support the investment proposition

Block reported $2.3B in gross profits for the fourth-quarter, showing 14% year-over-year growth. In Cash App, gross profits grew at an annual rate of 16% to $1.4B. Square -- a segment that is mainly concerned with providing point-of-sale technology to retail merchants -- saw 12% gross profit growth year-over-year to $924M. Both segments are therefore continuing to grow at double-digits, which is clearly indicative of a strong Fintech value proposition for Block's customers.

Within Cash App, a key driver for the Fintech has been growing adoption of Block’s Cash App Card, which is an easy-to-use debit card that allows users to immediately access the growing scale of the Cash App ecosystem. There are two factors that are driving growth for Cash App: 1) A higher number of Cash App Card users, and 2) Customers moving more funds into the Cash App ecosystem.

As to the first point, Cash App Card had 57M active users as of the end of FY 2024, showing only 2% year-over-year growth. In the year-earlier period, Block's Cash App Card users grew 9% Y/Y so the Fintech's growth here, in terms of Cash App Card adoption, has clearly slowed. However, Block’s customers moved $283B into the Cash App ecosystem in FY 2024, showing 14% year-over-year growth, so while user growth as moderated, the Cash App system has been able to continually attract a large (and growing) volume of capital.

Strong gross profit trends in both core segments -- Block and Square -- have resulted in an improving profitability profile as measured by the Fintech’s adjusted EBITDA. In Q4’24, the Fintech generated $757M in adjusted EBITDA, showing 35% year-over-year growth as well as a 33% margin. In the quarter-earlier period, Block's EBITDA margin was 36%, so the Fintech has, on a percentage basis, seen a small deterioration in its profitability.

Still, Block has been widely profitable in absolute dollar terms and should be able to continue to grow its gross profits and EBITDA in FY 2025. As per the Fintech's outlook for FY 2025, Block expects $10.2B in total gross profits, showing 15% Y/Y growth.

Block’s valuation

Block is expected to keep growing quickly, mainly due to strong gross profit momentum we have seen in the Cash App segment. Block’s projected annual EPS growth in FY 2026 is 24% Y/Y while PayPal (PYPL) is expected to grow only at half this rate, 12%. SoFi Technologies (SOFI), which is benefiting from very impressive customer acquisition, is projected to grow its earnings 103% in FY 2026.

Shares of Block are currently trading at a price-to-earnings ratio of 12.3X, while PayPal is trading at a P/E ratio of 13.3X and SoFi is priced at a 31.6X forward P/E ratio. Block's valuation could double from here, and shares would only be as expensive as they were on average in the last three years.

SoFi's growth, although more expensive, is worth the price, mainly because the Fintech is facing significant growth prospects and the platform just turned profitable: SoFi Is A Top Fintech Growth Play In 2025. PayPal, on the other hand, is cheap due to investors' concerns about growth and increasing competition in the digital wallet market. I still like PayPal, however, as the Fintech runs an exceptionally profitable platform based off of free cash flow. PayPal also just approved a $15.0B stock buyback, which provides additional value for investors.

In my last work on Block, I calculated a fair value of ~$112 per-share, based off of a historical P/E ratio of 25X and a consensus FY 2025 estimate of $4.45 per-share... which is the exact value that I confirmed after Block's fourth-quarter earnings report. Currently, shares of the Fintech trade for about half this longer-term valuation average, indicating that investors are getting a very good bargain on the drop.

Risks with Block

Block is still expected to deliver strong and consistent EPS and gross profit growth in the next several years, which is why consider the sell-off to be exaggerated. Slowing product adoption with regard to the Cash App Card is a potential risk factor for investors, but Block's outlook for FY 2025, in terms of gross profits, shows a healthily growing Fintech brand. What would change my mind about Block is if the company were to see slowing gross profit growth and falling capital inflows in the Cash App segment.

Final thoughts

Block submitted a decent earnings report card for the fourth-quarter, despite missing top and bottom line estimates. The Fintech again showed considerable strength in gross profit growth across Block's businesses, but especially in the Cash App ecosystem. Cash App users are growing, although at more moderate rates, and the Fintech is seeing a strong gross profit and EBITDA profile. Shares of Block are cheap after Friday's exaggerated 18% drop, allowing investors to buy into the Fintech at a fairly reasonable valuation, given its impressive growth profile.

data.title: Bridgestone: Strategically Sound With Good Traction
data.description:
Synopsis

A small position in Bridgestone Corporation (OTCPK:BRDCY) (OTCPK:BRDCF) may be warranted in your investment portfolio. Bridgestone is a dominant player in the global tire market, with a long-standing reputation for producing high-quality, premium tires. The company is well positioned in both consumer and commercial markets, benefiting from strong brand recognition and commitment to innovation. Bridgestone is proactively adapting to a rapidly transforming automotive market, including the rise in battery electric vehicles (BEVs) and sustainability-focused initiatives. Bridgestone maintains a robust balance sheet, supported by stable revenue growth and a disciplined cost structure. A discounted free cash flow (DFCF) analysis suggests that the company is fairly valued, making it an investment opportunity worth considering.

In this article, I will first review Bridgestone’s core business and market share. I will then share how I believe Bridgestone is innovating with new technology and is appropriately adapting to shifting markets and challenges. Next, I will examine the company’s financial performance and balance sheet, and I will value the company using a discounted free cash flow model. Last, as with any investment, I’ll highlight the most pronounced risks behind my investment thesis.

Core Business and Market Share

Bridgestone Corporation is one of the world’s leading tire manufacturers, with a strong global presence across multiple business segments. The company operates in four primary divisions: (i) their core Tire and Rubber Business which encompasses passenger, truck, and specialty tires; (ii) Diversified Products which includes a range of non-tire rubber products such as conveyor belts, hoses, and anti-vibration solutions; (iii) their Solutions Business that integrates digital technology, predictive analytics, and tire-as-a-service models for fleet management; and (iv) their Sports and Cycle Segment that produces golf equipment and bicycles and represents a small portion of overall revenue.

Tire sales represent the largest fraction driver for Bridgestone’s revenue. As shown in Figure 1, the company maintains a leading position in the global tire market, competing closely with Michelin and Goodyear. While Michelin has emphasized sustainability and technological advancements, and Goodyear has focused on OEM partnerships, Bridgestone differentiates itself through its premium offerings and, to some extent, its strategic expansion into digital solutions. Its global distribution network and focus on high-performance products have made it a key player in the industry.

In the US, Bridgestone accounts for an estimated 15-18% of all tire sales. Further, these tires are largely produced inside the US, reducing the potential impact of Trump-imposed tariffs. From Bridgestone’s perspective, their tire sales in the US account for about one third of their total revenue.

Innovation Targets

Tires sound simple, but they are loaded with technology. Premium tires are not commodity products, and tire quality varies greatly from brand to brand. Premium tires require good grip, handling, and braking in various conditions, low rolling resistance, and durability. Further, the aim is to deliver these qualities at reduced costs; and sometimes a technological breakthrough is not about performance, but it is about reducing costs. I’ll highlight three examples that show how Bridgestone is committed to innovative technology and reducing costs.

From a carbon circularity standpoint, Bridgestone is betting that tire recycling will play an important role in the future. The company is approaching tire recycling through its precise pyrolysis technology. Imagine making use of worn out tires rather than paying a fee for their disposal. Bridgestone’s approach is to break down used tires into high quality raw materials such as pyrolysis oil and carbon black that can be used in new tire production. Bridgestone has shown that this is possible at the pilot-scale, and they are now working toward full scale commercialization. If successful, this could be win-win because it saves raw materials costs while satisfying customers who demand carbon circularity. Bridgestone’s long-term goal is to achieve 100% sustainable materials usage by 2050.

Also, related to sustainability, Bridgestone’s Virtual Tire Program will allow engineers to test tire performance in a digital environment before actually producing physical prototypes. This practice can accelerate tire development while saving hundreds of prototypes for each new tire design.

My last example of innovation is perhaps the most important in terms of generating future revenue. We are witnessing the transformation from internal combustion automobiles to EVs. While this trend may be slowing in Europe, the U.S. BEV market is poised for significant growth. BEVs are expected to account for about 13.5% of US sales in 2025 with the figure growing to nearly 40% by 2035.

EVs are heavier, generate more torque, and put tires under greater stain. Moreover, customers demand longer battery range; and, for tires, this translates to a demand for low rolling resistance. Customers driving EVs will be far better off with premium tires that meet these needs. As electric vehicles (EVs) continue to grow in market share, companies that can produce premium EV tires at scale will benefit. Some of Bridgestone’s own Turanza’s brands use ENLITEN technology with reinforced tire structures, advanced tread compounds, and low rolling resistance. Figure 2 shows spider charts that illustrate progress made in developing Turanza’s tires for BEVs.

Financial Performance & Balance Sheet

Bridgestone’s income statement for the first half of fiscal 2024 shows modest revenue growth alongside mixed profitability. Total revenue rose 4% year‐on‐year—driven by an improved sales mix in premium tire segments despite some overall sales volume declines. Meanwhile, adjusted operating profit slipped, narrowing the margin from 11.3% to 10.5%.

On the balance sheet, Bridgestone has strengthened its financial position over the past few years. Figure 3 shows how the company’s capital structure has evolved with time; the y-axis has been normalized by Bridgestone’s market capitalization to enable clear visualization of how a dollar invested lands on the balance sheet. For example, Bridgestone carries nearly one dollar of equity per dollar invested; this is consistent with the firm’s low price to book value of 1.11 taken from Seeking Alpha. Figure 3 also shows that total assets have steadily grown, and the company has kept a good handle on interest-bearing debt—underscoring effective capital management in a challenging market environment.

The contrast between Bridgestone's and Goodyear's (GT) balance sheets is striking. Goodyear has even more equity-- over 150% of their market capitalization-- but they also carry enormous debt, with a debt-to-equity ratio of nearly 180%. This makes Goodyear, in my opinion, a riskier investment than Bridgestone.

Intrinsic Valuation

Bridgestone’s intrinsic value was estimated using a discounted free cash flow (DFCF) model. Key assumptions are: (i) revenue growth rate for the next three years is 3.2% based on analysts' projections, and, after that, revenue is assumed to grow at 3.0% per year; (ii) the effective tax rate is taken to be 27.0%, based on historical averages; (iii) future EBIT margins are assumed to be 10.0% based on analyst projections; and (iv) a discount rate of 7.9% was used based on my own calculation of the weighted average cost of capital. Figure 4 shows a plot of revenue, EBIT, and EBITDA projections. Further, also based on historical data, the company’s PPE is assumed to depreciate at 14% per year, and capital expenditure is budgeted to cover 80% of depreciated loss as well as projected growth. The 80% adjustment is intended for capex to only cover maintenance capex (for the unlevered analysis), and the number is derived from historical data. Since as much as 30% of Bridgestone’s revenue is tied up as working capital, based on a 10-year average, the estimated free cash flow streams were further reduced by this factor. The model was conducted using an unlevered basis, which is appropriate since the company has very little debt.

The DFCF model indicates that Bridgestone has an intrinsic value of $17 which is a few dollars below the current stock price. However, considering the company’s sizable market share, strong balance sheet, and strategic investments, the company appears reasonably valued.

Key Risks

Bridgestone faces significant risks that could impact its profitability and investment appeal. A key challenge is the continued underperformance of its Latin American operations, particularly in Brazil and Argentina. Despite Bridgestone’s stabilization efforts, Brazil remains unprofitable due to an influx of low-cost tire imports, while Argentina’s economic instability adds further uncertainty. These losses have weighed on the company’s overall earnings based on their first half of 2024 report.

Another risk is intensifying competition from low-cost tire imports, especially in North America and Brazil. Budget brands from China, Thailand, and Vietnam have entered the market, undercutting Bridgestone’s pricing power. While Bridgestone continues to push its premium segment, consumers are increasingly opting for cheaper alternatives, eroding market share, and forcing a reliance on the Firestone brand to stay competitive. However, as pointed out in the previous Seeking Alpha analyst article on Bridgestone, US tariffs on imports may actually ease competition, i.e., since most of Bridgestone’s tires sold in the US are produced in the US, Bridgestone should be rather unaffected by US tariffs.

Bridgestone in the midst of a complex and multi-year restructuring effort, with a focus on cutting fixed costs, optimizing production, and closing underperforming retail locations. However, the success of these efforts remains uncertain. Delays or inefficiencies in execution could limit the expected profitability improvements, making it harder for Bridgestone to meet its 2025-2026 financial targets​.

Take-home Message

In consideration of Bridgestone’s large market share, strategic focus on premium segments, innovation, and cost optimization efforts make it a compelling part of a long-term portfolio for investors seeking stability in the tire industry. While competition from low-cost tire imports, fluctuating raw material prices, and economic pressures in key markets such as Latin America present challenges, Bridgestone is fairly valued and is taking appropriate steps to address these challenges.

data.title: Innovative Industrial Properties: Dividend Yield Is Safe Amidst Elevated Liquidity
data.description:
Innovative Industrial Properties (NYSE:IIPR) trading hands for under $80 per share would have seemed unfathomable at the start of 2024, but is now the reality for shareholders following the December default of its largest tenant. PharmaCann comprised roughly 17% of annualized base rent in the REIT's fiscal 2024 third quarter before the default. The default came as a shock as the private multi-state operator was broadly well capitalized. PharmaCann defaulted on its obligations to pay rent under six of its eleven leases with IIPR for properties spread across six states. At risk is IIPR's dividend, with the yield on the industrial cannabis REIT being driven to a record double-digit.

IIPR last declared a quarterly cash dividend of $1.90 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution and $7.60 per share annualized for a 10.4% dividend yield. This essentially forms a record dividend yield for IIPR and comes at a material divergence from the REIT's historical averages, even as liquidity stays stable on strong free cash flow generation. IIPR's 4-year average dividend yield stands at 5.83%, with the current yield a material 457 basis points higher. This comes even as IIPR enjoys an adjusted fund from operations yield that's 76% higher than its sector group median.

Liquidity, Risks, PharmaCann, And Free Cash Flow

IIPR ended its fiscal 2024 fourth quarter with cash and cash equivalents of $146.2 million, up $6 million from its year-ago comp. The REIT generated fourth-quarter revenue of $76.74 million, a slight 3.1% dip over its year-ago comp but a beat by $530,000 on consensus estimates. IIPR generated fourth-quarter funds from operations ("FFO") of $2.03 per share, a 4 cents beat on consensus estimates. This figure also grew by 2 cents sequentially from the third quarter, but was down from $2.07 per share a year ago.

Hence, IIPR is covering its dividend by 106%, a roughly 94% payout ratio. The REIT's trailing-12-month free cash flow trendline has also broadly always covered total dividends paid, which includes its 9.00% Series A Cumulative Redeemable Preferreds (NYSE:IIPR.PR.A). The market is now spooked by the underlying credit quality of IIPR's tenants, even with the REIT's robust FFO and free cash flow profile. Critically, IIPR held a total debt balance of just $300 million as of the end of the fourth quarter. These fixed-rate unsecured bonds mature in May 2026 with the REIT likely using excess free cash flow, its credit facility and cash, or a future refinancing transaction to address its maturity.

IIPR has one of the least geared balance sheets of a public REIT, with an 11% debt-to-total gross assets ratio and a 17x debt service coverage ratio as of the end of its fourth quarter. Further, there has also been a resolution of the PharmaCann lease that materially diminishes the prospects of a dividend cut, with the risk facing the REIT coalesced around whether more IIPR tenants will face near-term difficulty. IIPR's low debt balance presents a significant tailwind for management, as it means more financial flexibility.

IIPR has since amended its lease with PharmaCann which will see the cannabis MSO pay continue to lease the original nine properties, spread across 434,000 square feet, but with a reduced cumulative total base rent. PharmaCann will now pay $2.6 million per month, down $200,000 from its prior base of $2.8 million per month, with cash rent payments commencing from the first of February. The 7.14% dip in base rent is extremely manageable and poses no risk to IIPR's quarterly dividend. Hence, the elevated dividend yield, on the back of 100 basis points of Fed rate cuts in the second half of 2024, seems unhinged. Bears would be right to flag risks from a moribund cannabis rescheduling push as a possible further source of negative investor sentiment. We could see a further selloff in the industry if the entire process is pulled by the new administration.

IIPR's operating portfolio was 98.3% leased as of the end of the fourth quarter, up 260 basis points sequentially from 95.7% in the third quarter. The REIT's $87.5 million revolving credit facility also remained undrawn at the end of December. The preferreds offer a safer choice for investors looking to gain some exposure to the industry, with their $2.25 per share coupon currently driving an 8.67% yield on cost. It's important to note that these are trading above their $25 liquidation value per share by roughly 95 cents, a 3.8% premium that reflects the high headline coupon. This keeps me away from them, especially with a yield that's lower than the commons. IIPR's commons form a strong buy, with the preferreds a hold.

Fund performance

Columbia Disciplined Growth Fund Class A shares returned 4.33% (excluding sales charge) and its Institutional shares returned 4.41% for the quarter ended December 31, 2024. The fund posted positive absolute returns but underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 1000 Growth Index (Russell Index), which returned 7.07% for the same period. The fund’s results can be attributed primarily to the relative performance of its model’s stock selection measures.

Market overview

The U.S. stock market gained 2.75% in the fourth quarter, based on the large-cap Russell 1000 Index, bringing its return for the full year to 24.51%. The November election result removed a long-standing source of uncertainty for investors, and it raised hopes that government policy would shift in a more pro-growth, market-friendly direction. In addition, U.S. equities continued to attract investor capital as the nation’s economic growth remained well above that of its global developed-market peers. These factors helped propel stocks to a positive return despite indications that the U.S. Federal Reserve is unlikely to cut interest rates significantly in the year ahead.

The shift in expectations toward a less accommodative Fed played a role in the continued outperformance of growth stocks. As was the case throughout 2024, U.S. mega-cap technology companies — particularly those expected to capitalize on the increased adoption of artificial intelligence — remained the primary driver of both broader market returns and relative strength of the growth style. The Russell 1000 Growth Index returned 7.07% in the quarter, outpacing the -1.98% return of the Russell 1000 Value Index. The two indices returned 33.36% and 14.37%, respectively, for the full year.

In another continuation of a trend that was in place in the year’s first three quarters, large-cap stocks outperformed mid-caps (as measured by the 0.62% return of the Russell MidCap Index) and small caps (as measured by the 0.33% return of the Russell 2000 Index). Smaller companies are typically more rate-sensitive than large caps, and they benefited less from the robust inflows into passive investments.

Model performance

We divide the metrics for our stock selection model into three broad categories — quality, value and catalyst. We then rank the securities within a sector/industry from “1” (most attractive) to “5” (least attractive) based upon the metrics within these categories. During the fourth quarter of 2024, the catalyst theme contributed positively to relative results, but the quality and value themes detracted. The value theme of our stock selection model was weakest, with all eight of its factors dampening relative results during the quarter. Dividend yield, earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization (EBITDA)-to-enterprise value and forward earnings/price were the largest detractors within the value theme and overall. The quality theme of our stock selection model had mixed performance, with three of its factors contributing positively and three detracting. The change in free-cash-flow margin and cash-flow-return on assets factors performed well, while the return-on-equity and return-on-invested capital factors weighed on results. Within the catalyst theme, three of its four factors scored positively during the quarter, led by the best-performing factor overall, cash flow surprise. The earnings revision and 12-month price momentum factors also performed well, with relative strength the only catalyst factor to score negatively during the quarter.

Among risk factors, volatility and beta were the largest positive contributors to relative results for the quarter, while financial leverage and size were the largest detractors.

Of the portfolio’s 20 industry-specific models, eight provided positive stock selection guidance during the fourth quarter. Utilities, health care-services and consumer discretionary-retail & apparel contributed most positively to relative results. Consumer discretionary-autos & durables, energy and health care-products detracted the most during the quarter.

As usual, the portfolio maintained a relatively neutral stance on sector allocation, though sector allocation did detract, albeit modestly, from relative performance during the quarter. Stock selection overall detracted the most from the portfolio’s performance relative to the Russell Index. Stock selection was weakest in the consumer discretionary, communication services and industrials sectors. These detractors were partially offset by effective stock selection in information technology, real estate and materials, which contributed positively to relative results.

Top detractors from relative performance

  • Tesla (TSLA), an electric vehicle maker, ended the quarter with a robust double-digit share price gain due primarily to a November election boost due to Elon Musk’s connection to the incoming U.S. President. However, the portfolio held an underweight in Tesla because of its poor value and quality theme scores, and the stock proved to be a significant detractor from relative results.
  • NVR, a homebuilding company that also offers mortgage banking and title services, experienced a double-digit share price decline during the quarter. The homebuilding industry broadly pulled back in the last months of 2024, as demand trends had yet to improve meaningfully and mortgage rates moved higher, both serving as headwinds. The portfolio’s out-of-Russell Index position in NVR was based on positive scores in the catalyst and quality themes, but the models delivered negative stock selection guidance.

Importantly, a notable portion of the fund’s relative underperformance was driven by its underweighted allocations to several mega-cap stocks, which were the best-performing segment of the U.S. equity market during the quarter, given SEC-mandated structural restrictions on position size as a percentage of portfolio net assets.

Top positive contributors to relative performance

  • Fortinet (FTNT), a cybersecurity company, enjoyed a double-digit share price gain for a second consecutive quarter. These results were primarily driven by COVID-based hardware constraint costs coming to an end and the business shifting more to software and services. The portfolio’s overweight in Fortinet was due to attractive quality and catalyst theme scores, and the models delivered positive stock selection guidance.
  • Microsoft, the software giant, saw its shares dip during the quarter, as investors focused on disappointing revenue from its cloud-based business segment, even as the company reported better-than-expected earnings and revenue overall. However, the portfolio had an underweight position in Microsoft, which led to it being a top positive contributor to relative results.

During the fourth quarter, certain enhancements were made to our quantitative stock selection models to improve their relevance in uncertain markets. Most notably, the adjusted book-to-price factor was enhanced to now use combined trailing and forward return-on-equity data. Extreme factor values are managed by applying percentile ranks pre-regression. (Regression models are a tool for predictive analytics used in quantitative modeling.)

Outlook

For the second consecutive year, U.S. equity markets rallied substantially, with the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) hitting 57 new all-time highs in 2024 alone. Given this strong run in the markets, the foremost question on investors’ minds is whether the rally still has legs or if there will be causes for it to stall. We believe the actions of the incoming U.S. administration, the Federal Reserve’s interest-rate policy and the resilience of the consumer, among other drivers, will likely determine which direction the markets take.

The new year will bring a new administration to Washington, D.C., and with it, substantial changes in government policy. With support in both the House and the Senate, there appears to be a higher chance the administration can deliver on many of its promises. An emphasis on deregulation and a focus on economic growth and energy production are likely, in our opinion, to be tailwinds to corporate profitability. A friendlier approach to corporate mergers and acquisitions may also be a positive for the equity markets. On the other hand, the incoming President’s focus on tariffs, especially with close trading partners Canada and Mexico, could dampen economic activity and increase inflation. Potential trade wars can create more friction to global commerce.

Investors will almost surely continue to watch if this is a serious policy proposal or just a negotiating tactic.

The Fed, having navigated the historic rise in inflation with aggressive interest rate increases, began easing rates in September 2024 and seems to have successfully avoided a hard economic landing. As the last mile of the inflation reduction journey remains challenging, Fed policymakers have been reconsidering the pace and trajectory of interest rate cuts for the months ahead. However, despite inflation remaining stubborn at around 3%, there also seems to be consensus among Fed officials that the neutral rate — or the interest rate that would prevail when the economy is at full employment and inflation is stable — is lower than current interest rates. So, we believe we are likely to see interest rate cuts in 2025 that can support equity markets.

High levels of and rising delinquency rates in consumer debt and auto loans could be a cause for concern, in our view, but spending has remained robust, and lower interest rates should provide additional cushion to shore up the health of the consumer.

Similarly, in the housing market, where high mortgage rates have significantly affected the supply of existing homes for sale, we feel a reduction in rates should catalyze more transactions and continue to support this sector of the economy. The labor market remained in balance at the end of 2024, with unemployment hovering around 4%.

Despite these positives, with the economy in 2024 growing at a faster clip than consensus expectations, we believe a slight slowdown is likely in the next year.

One more theme we believe will continue to be important going forward is the potential of Artificial Intelligence adoption to transform businesses. The largest companies have invested hundreds of billions of dollars in capital expenditure to compete for new revenue opportunities. As the returns from these investments become clearer, markets may well reward those with the best strategy and execution. Geopolitical risk also remains a concern for the U.S. equity markets, with conflicts in the Middle East and Ukraine still simmering.

The effects of government policy, Fed actions and other market drivers are likely, in our opinion, to be uneven across industries, and foreseeing which market segments will lead or lag can be challenging. Just as we observed in the fourth quarter of 2024, there could be wide dispersion among the prospects of different economic sectors. Focusing on stock selection and staying relatively sector neutral can mitigate some performance volatility. Relying on fundamentals to identify those companies that can execute on their strategies and post solid earnings and cash flows can also be a potential benefit to portfolio results. Amid such an environment, we believe our multifactor approach, focusing collectively on quality, value and catalyst themes, should put us in good stead in selecting stocks. It remains important to note that our strategy is based on individual quantitative stock selection. Consequently, we do not rely on macroeconomic scenarios or market outlooks to make security selections. We do not try to predict when equities, as an asset class, may perform well or when they may perform poorly. Instead, we keep the portfolio substantially invested at all times. Our portfolio managers maintain confidence in the long-term efficacy of our investment approach.
